name: Structure Me
bio: Working notes from Structure Me on corporate structures, jurisdictions, and succession.

title: Trusts, companies and hybrids: choosing the structure your business will sell from
dek: A working paper on the three corporate architectures most Australian founders choose between — and how the decision you make in year one quietly decides the price you achieve in year ten.
metaTitle: Trusts, Companies and Hybrids: Choosing the Structure Your Business Will Sell From | Structure Me
metaDescription: A working paper on the three corporate architectures most Australian founders choose between — discretionary trusts, proprietary companies, and hybrid arrangements — and how the choice you make at year one quietly decides the price you achieve at year ten.
metaKeywords: business structure Australia, discretionary trust vs company, hybrid structure, business sale structure, asset protection structure, corporate structuring Melbourne, family trust business

children.text: Most Australian founders we meet have already chosen the wrong structure. Not catastrophically — the business still runs, the tax still gets paid — but quietly, expensively, in ways that compound over a decade. The structure is wrong because it was chosen by their first accountant, in their first year, against a brief that no longer exists.
The choice is almost always between three architectures: a
— typically a company operating beneath a trust, or a unit trust held by a family trust. Each is right for something. None is right for everything.
Choose the structure that protects the business you have today and does not get in the way of the business you will sell tomorrow. That is the whole brief. Everything below is the unpacking of it.

takeaways: The first structure is rarely the last — design for the business you'll sell, not just the one you operate.
Discretionary trusts give flexibility; companies give simplicity; hybrids give both — at a cost.
The decision is harder, not easier, once the business is succeeding.
